planting.note: Plant outdoors to a shaded location after temperatures remain above 45°F; in zone 9 plant March–May. In zone 9a Xanadu Philodendron is borderline hardy — the rhizome may survive light frost when mulched, but temperatures below 28°F will kill the above-ground foliage. Manage as a container plant on a shaded porch or patio and bring indoors when frost threatens, or plant in the ground in the most frost-protected garden microclimate. In zones 10–11 it grows as a fully hardy landscape shrub reaching 4 feet by 5 feet. The deeply lobed leaves create a refined tropical effect in shaded outdoor living spaces, and the plant handles zone 9's summer heat and humidity well if maintained with consistent moisture.

directions: Plant in partial to deep shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moist soil — Xanadu Philodendron thrives in the shade conditions of tropical forest understories, performing best where direct sun is excluded; Xanadu (Thaumatophyllum xanadu, formerly Philodendron xanadu) is a compact, self-heading philodendron producing a dramatically architectural mound of deeply pinnately lobed, dark glossy green leaves that create a refined, sophisticated tropical texture at a manageable scale — growing to a tidy 2–4 foot mound rather than the sprawling, climbing habit of many philodendron species. Each large leaf (up to 18 inches long on established plants) is divided into deeply cut lobes along the length that create a feathery, textured appearance fundamentally different from the entire or shallowly lobed leaves of most shade tropicals. Xanadu's architectural foliage — the precise, repeating cut-leaf pattern, the glossy dark surface, the mounding form — creates a modern, clean aesthetic that works well in contemporary landscape design as well as in tropical garden styles. As an outdoor shade plant for zone 9 patios and covered garden areas, Xanadu provides a tropical character with more refined foliage texture than elephant ears or cannas, creating a sophisticated shade garden texture suitable for formal as well as casual landscape settings.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Thaumatophyllum xanadu for Florida Landscapes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Shade Foliage for Gulf South Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Philodendron Species for Zone 9 Outdoor Use

planting.note: Move outdoors to a deep shade location after temperatures remain above 60°F; in zone 9 place outdoors April–October and bring indoors when temperatures approach 50°F. Chinese evergreen is among the most sensitive common tropical foliage plants to cold — chilling injury occurs at temperatures below 50°F and frost kills plants immediately. Zone 9 summers (May–October) provide ideal conditions for outdoor display on shaded patios and in covered garden areas. Keep out of direct sun at all times — even brief exposure to direct midday sun in zone 9 causes immediate, irreversible leaf bleaching and burn. Leaf sap is a skin irritant in some individuals.

directions: Plant in deep shade to bright indirect light — never in direct sun — in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, consistently moist soil; Silver Queen is one of the classic Chinese Evergreen cultivars, producing lance-shaped leaves with a distinctive silver-green variegation pattern — a silver-gray to cream central area on each leaf with darker green margins and irregular darker green markings that create a subtle, sophisticated foliage pattern. Chinese Evergreen (Aglaonema) is one of the most adaptable and tolerant foliage plants for low-light conditions — genuinely thriving in the deep shade conditions where most other ornamental plants cannot survive or maintain acceptable appearance. Silver Queen's tolerance of genuinely low light levels, combined with the refined, silver-variegated foliage and relatively compact scale, makes it one of the most practically useful plants for the specific challenge of decorating and greening shaded spaces — covered porches, pergolas with overhead cover, north-facing entries, and any situation where light levels are insufficient for most ornamental plants but where lush, attractive foliage is desired. As an outdoor container plant in zone 9's humid summers, Chinese Evergreen thrives in the warm, humid conditions on shaded patios that replicate its native Southeast Asian forest understory habitat.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Aglaonema as Florida Interior and Exterior Foliage
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Shade Container Plants for Zone 9 Patios
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tropical Foliage Plants for Gulf Coast Indoor-Outdoor Use

planting.note: Move outdoors to a shaded location after temperatures remain above 60°F; in zone 9 place outdoors April–October and overwinter indoors. Sensation Peace Lily is the largest widely available peace lily cultivar — capable of reaching 4–5 feet tall with leaves 24–36 inches long. Maintain consistently moist soil; peace lily wilts dramatically when dry but recovers quickly when watered. The white spathe-and-spadix flowers appear 1–3 times per year on well-grown plants. Outdoors in zone 9's summer heat and humidity, Sensation performs exceptionally well in deep shade on covered patios. Leaf edges show brown tips if exposed to fluoride in tap water — use filtered or rainwater for best appearance.

directions: Plant in deep shade to bright indirect light — direct sun bleaches and burns the large, dark green leaves irreparably — in well-drained, humus-rich, consistently moist, slightly acidic soil; Sensation is the large-growing selection of Peace Lily (Spathiphyllum), producing plants two to three times the size of standard peace lily cultivars — creating a dramatic, statement-scale tropical foliage plant for large containers, shaded patios, and indoor spaces with high ceilings. The very large, lance-shaped, deeply ribbed, dark glossy green leaves reach 24–36 inches on mature established plants, creating a bold, lush tropical effect that is impossible to achieve with standard-sized Spathiphyllum varieties. The flowers — pure white, calla lily-like spathes surrounding a cream-white spadix — appear intermittently through the year on well-maintained plants, adding a refined, elegant floral dimension to the dramatic foliage. Peace lily in general is one of the definitive shade and low-light plants — one of very few flowering plants that blooms in genuinely low light conditions — and Sensation simply amplifies all these qualities to a scale appropriate for dramatic indoor or outdoor shaded garden design.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Spathiphyllum Species for Florida Interior and Shade Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Large-Scale Shade Tropicals for Gulf South Patios
American Horticultural Society — Spathiphyllum Large Cultivar Selection

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Little Henry is a dwarf compact selection (18–24 inches) of Virginia Sweetspire — far smaller than the straight species (3–5 feet) and better suited for low foundation plantings, border edges, and small garden spaces. It suckers slowly to form a colony; remove unwanted suckers to maintain a compact clump. In zone 9 the outstanding fall color (burgundy to orange-red to crimson, lasting weeks into November) is the most dramatic ornamental feature. Tolerates wet soils and periodically flooded conditions better than most ornamental shrubs.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Little Henry Virginia Sweetspire performs well in the partial shade typical of the Gulf South understory, with good color and flowering in conditions too shaded for many ornamental shrubs; Little Henry (sold under the trade name 'Little Henry', patent name 'Sprich') is a compact selection of Virginia Sweetspire (Itea virginica), a native deciduous shrub of the Gulf South coastal plain that provides three distinct seasons of ornamental interest — fragrant white bottlebrush flower spikes in June–July, healthy green summer foliage, and an extraordinary fall color display of burgundy, orange-red, and crimson that persists for weeks longer than most shrubs in zone 9's mild autumns. The fragrant white flower spikes (4–6 inches long) appear in June when relatively few other ornamental shrubs bloom, attracting pollinators during a midsummer gap in the landscape flowering sequence. As a native Gulf Coast species, Virginia Sweetspire is perfectly adapted to the heat, humidity, and periodic flooding of Gulf South landscapes — tolerating wet soils along pond edges, creek banks, and low-lying areas where introduced landscape shrubs often fail. The compact scale of Little Henry makes the ornamental quality of the species accessible for residential landscapes where the spreading colonizing habit of the straight species would be inappropriate.
sources: North Carolina State University Extension — Itea virginica 'Little Henry' Plant Profile
University of Georgia Extension — Virginia Sweetspire for Southern Native Landscapes
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Itea virginica Native Plant

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Red Autumn Sage is a hardy, long-lived perennial shrub in zone 9 — established plants can persist for 10+ years with minimal care. Prune to 6–8 inches in late winter to remove dead wood and stimulate fresh, densely flowering new growth; without annual pruning plants become leggy and produce fewer flowers. In zone 9 blooms most heavily in spring (March–May) and fall (September–November) — flowering is reduced during the heat of July–August but rarely stops entirely. The small, tubular, vivid red flowers are one of the premier hummingbird nectar sources among all landscape plants.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil — Red Autumn Sage is a Texas limestone native demanding excellent drainage and thriving in the lean, dry conditions that produce the most abundant flowering; Red Autumn Sage (Salvia greggii) is a small evergreen shrub native to the Chihuahuan Desert region of Texas and Mexico that has become one of the most valuable and widely planted ornamental sages for zones 7–9 — providing reliable, continuous flowering from spring through fall in the heat, drought, and alkaline soil conditions where most ornamental plants require intensive support. The vivid scarlet to cherry-red tubular flowers are produced in dense spikes on arching stems in successive flushes through the growing season, with the richest flowering in the cool-weather periods of spring and fall in zone 9. Salvia greggii is one of the most reliably effective hummingbird plants in the Gulf South — the tubular red flowers are specifically adapted to hummingbird pollination and attract Ruby-throated Hummingbirds and other hummingbird species wherever they occur in the plant's range. The combination of drought tolerance, heat tolerance, alkaline soil adaptation, compact evergreen habit, continuous bloom, and hummingbird value makes Red Autumn Sage effectively irreplaceable as a low-maintenance, wildlife-supporting perennial for the warmer, drier parts of zones 7–9.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Salvia greggii for Texas Landscapes
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Salvia greggii Native Plant Profile
University of Texas at Austin Native Plant Society — Autumn Sage Cultivation Guide
